text: The surgeon begins a robotic sigmoid colectomy, focusing on the colovascular fistula visible adjacent to the bladder. The medial to lateral dissection is initiated, revealing the retroperitoneal plane despite lateral inflammatory reactions. The ureter becomes visible as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon thins out the pedicle around the superior hemorrhoidal artery, preparing for ligation. The bowel wall is assessed for length, and the surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to enhance visualization of the ureters, facilitating faster identification and dissection.

The surgeon performs further lateral dissection using a thirty-degree scope, successfully reaching the white line from the medial side. A sponge is placed to protect the ureter, and the surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection. The bowel rotation is hindered by the fistula, prompting its removal.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the benign fistula, preserving the bladder by staying close to the colon. The bleeding granulation tissue of the fistula is visible. The surgeon navigates around the left adnexa, which is drawn into the area, and approaches from the right side. The fallopian tube is identified, and the uterus is suspended to enhance visibility. Further lateral dissection is performed to connect with the medial plane, revealing the gonadal vessels and the ureter, with a sponge placed for protection.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure and creates a window around the fallopian tube, revealing the gonadal vessels and ovaries plastered to the colon. The anterior dissection between the uterus and colon is performed, allowing visualization of the left TME plane. This facilitates mobilization of the upper rectum beneath the inflammatory reaction to reach healthy rectum for anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies an old endometriotic cyst and exposes the healthy mid rectum. The splenic flexure has been mobilized without altering the patient's position or port configuration. The surgeon cleans the mid rectum, preparing for specimen extraction without morselation due to the lack of a preoperative colonoscopy.

The surgeon performs a fan and steel extraction, using a stapler to divide the bowel. The mesentery is cleaned off the proximal bowel to ensure clear visibility of the bowel wall. ICG is checked with the stapler in place to confirm perfusion before stapling across the rectum. The anastomosis is completed with a sewn two-layer technique, starting with the inner row, sewing first to the left and then to the right side.

The surgeon performs canalization over the top of the bowel, using the original suture to imbricate over the posterior staple lines. The healthy bowel is prepared for anastomosis without diversion, and a leak test is conducted. The uterus is dropped, and the fistula is visually inspected.
